title: Olive Oil on Cotton
timeEstimate: 20–40 minutes; powder + Dawn, hot wash AFTER chemistry on white cotton
stainChemistry: Olive oil on cotton is the easy oil case — cotton has the widest home bench and tolerates the full toolkit (Dawn, hot wash AFTER chemistry, 3% peroxide on whites, bounded chlorine bleach on solid white cotton). The recurring traps are simple: water-first instead of powder-first (water spreads oil into a wider ring), and hot dryer with residual oil (polymerizes oil into the cotton as a permanent yellow shadow). Order: powder preload → Dawn → hot wash → AIR-DRY first time + inspect. Olive oil is slightly slower-oxidizing than vegetable oils so it's a bit more forgiving on aging, but hot-dried oil is the lock either way.
whyThisWorks: Powder preload pulls bulk oil up before water. Dawn dish soap is engineered for cooking grease — emulsifies olive oil cleanly. Hot wash is safe AFTER chemistry on cotton because the oil is gone. AIR-DRY first time prevents heat-locking residual oil into the cotton.
scienceNote: Mechanism (pro-facing): olive oil is mostly oleic acid (monounsaturated triglyceride). Slower to polymerize than polyunsaturated oils (canola, soybean) but heat-locks the same way. Pro uses POG / petroleum ether for oil + NSD + 3% H₂O₂ for residual chromophore on whites.
defaultAssumption: Cooking spatter or salad dressing drip on a cotton shirt or napkin. Fresh = quick win with powder + Dawn + hot wash. Hot-dried oil = often a yellow shadow; pro for valuable garments.

spottingProtocol.technique: Pull bulk oil up before water
spottingProtocol.instruction: Cover the spot with cornstarch or baking soda. Press gently. 20–30 min absorb. Soft-brush off. Most surface oil comes off this way without water.
Generous Dawn directly on the spot. Soft toothbrush 10 min. Cotton tolerates warm water. Damp-cloth rinse.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Washing machine, hot water (per care label)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Hot machine wash per care label. Safe AFTER Dawn. Cotton tolerates hot up to 200°F. Inspect before dryer.
AIR-DRY first time. Inspect in daylight. Faint yellow shadow = repeat the Dawn pass. NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — locks it into cotton as a permanent yellow shadow.

steps: 1. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 20–30 min, soft-brush off.
2. Generous Dawn pretreat, warm, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
3. Hot wash per care label — safe AFTER Dawn.
4. AIR-DRY first time, inspect, repeat Dawn if yellow shadow remains.
products: Cool to warm water for chemistry, hot for wash
professionalProtocol.temperature: warm Dawn dwell + hot wash AFTER
warnings: Powder BEFORE water — water-first spreads oil
NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — polymerizes into cotton permanently
Honest cap: hot-dried weeks-old yellowing sometimes plateaus

homeSolutions.agent: Powder First, Dawn Second, Hot Wash AFTER
homeSolutions.instruction: Olive oil on cotton is one of the friendlier oil stains because cotton has the widest home bench. The path: powder preload → Dawn → hot wash → AIR-DRY first time. Two hard rules: powder BEFORE water (water-first spreads oil into a wider ring), and NO hot dryer with residual oil (locks it into cotton as a yellow shadow).

homeSolutions.agent: Cornstarch or Baking Soda — 20 to 30 Minutes
homeSolutions.instruction: Cover the oil spot with cornstarch, baking soda, or plain baby powder. Press gently. 20 to 30 minutes. Soft-brush off.
Apply a generous amount of Dawn dish soap directly to the spot. Work in with a soft toothbrush for 10 minutes. Cotton tolerates warm water — it helps the oil release. Damp-cloth rinse.
Hot machine wash per care label. Safe at this point because the oil is gone — heat is finishing-side, not setting-side. Cotton tolerates hot up to 200°F.

homeSolutions.agent: AIR-DRY First Time, Inspect Before Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: AIR-DRY first time. Inspect in daylight. Faint yellow shadow remaining? Repeat the Dawn pass before any dryer heat. NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — heat polymerizes the oil into the cotton and the yellow shadow becomes permanent.

steps: 1. Powder BEFORE water. NO hot dryer with residual oil.
2. Cornstarch / baking soda preload 20–30 min, soft-brush off.
3. Generous Dawn pretreat, warm, 10 min, soft toothbrush.
4. Hot wash per care label — safe AFTER Dawn.
5. AIR-DRY first time, inspect, repeat Dawn if yellow shadow remains.
products: Cool to warm water for chemistry, hot for wash
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if hot dryer was used on residual oil for weeks (often permanent yellow shadow). Stop after 3 home cycles plateau.
warnings: Powder BEFORE water — water-first spreads oil
NEVER hot dryer with residual oil — polymerizes into cotton
Aged hot-dried yellowing sometimes plateaus — pro escalation

neverDo: Never water-first on oil stains — spreads them
Never hot dryer with residual oil — polymerizes into cotton
Never chlorine bleach on dyed cotton
fiberSensitivities: Solid white cotton: widest bench — Dawn, hot wash, 3% peroxide if needed
Cotton blend with polyester: cool only — heat sets oil into polyester
Tolerates hot wash up to 200°F AFTER chemistry

materialWarnings: Powder before water — water-first spreads oil
Hot wash is finishing-side AFTER Dawn, not setting-side
No hot dryer with residual oil — permanent yellow shadow
Olive oil polymerizes slower than vegetable oils but still heat-locks

escalation.when: Hot dryer used on residual oil for weeks (often permanent yellow shadow). Aged buildup over months. 3 home cycles plateau. Cotton blend with polyester (cool-only).
escalation.whatToTell: "Olive oil on [solid white / white-with-trim / colored / blend] cotton, age [fresh / hours / weeks of buildup], home-treated with [cornstarch preload + Dawn + hot wash + air-dry]. No hot dryer applied. [Residual yellow shadow]."
escalation.specialistType: professional dry cleaner with solvent spotting capability for set yellow on cotton

contextQuestions: Cooking spatter, salad dressing drip, or aged buildup?
Solid white cotton, colored, or blend?
Fresh, hours old, or weeks/months of hot-dried buildup?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Olive oil is the gentler oil case — slower oxidation than vegetable / canola oils means more forgiveness on aging. Cotton's widest-bench advantage applies. Most common home failure is reaching for a damp cloth on wet oil instead of doing the powder step first.
